children.title: The Arrival
children.content:
Mara stepped off the train into a city she no longer recognised. The platform smelled of rain and diesel, and somewhere beneath that, the ghost of something older—coal dust, perhaps, from an era her grandmother had spoken about in scattered fragments.

She pulled her coat tighter and looked for the exit sign. Around her, commuters moved with the practiced indifference of people who belonged somewhere. She did not belong here. She had not belonged anywhere for six years.

The message from her brother had said only: Come now. It matters.
children.synopsis: Mara arrives in the city after six years away, summoned by a cryptic message from her estranged brother Eli.
children.notes: Sets the tone. Mara's outsider status established immediately. Consider adding a detail about the suitcase she carries.
children.label: blue

snapshots.label: Before revision
snapshots.content:
Mara stepped off the train. The city looked different. She hadn't been here in years.

Her brother had sent a message. She didn't know what it meant.
snapshots.note: First draft version before adding sensory details

sceneMetadata.timelineNotes: The day Mara arrives. All subsequent events flow from this.
sceneMetadata.unansweredQuestions: What does Eli want? Why now?
sceneMetadata.whatChanged: Mara enters the city. Her comfortable absence ends.

children.title: Eli's Apartment
children.content:
Eli lived on the fourth floor of a building that looked like it had been forgotten by its owners and remembered only by the pigeons. The buzzer didn't work. Mara stood in the rain for three minutes before a man in a delivery uniform held the door open for her without asking who she was.

The stairwell smelled of mildew and something fried. On the third-floor landing, a child's bicycle was chained to the banister, one wheel missing.

She knocked. A long silence. Then the sound of the chain being drawn back, and Eli's face appeared in the gap—older, thinner, with eyes that kept moving to something over her shoulder.
children.synopsis: Mara reaches Eli's run-down apartment and they have their first tense reunion.
children.notes: The missing bicycle wheel is symbolic. Return to it later. Eli's paranoid eye movement needs payoff.
children.label: orange

snapshots.label: Original draft — building description only
snapshots.content: Eli's building was run-down. Mara had to wait for someone to let her in. She went upstairs and knocked.

sceneMetadata.unansweredQuestions: What is Eli afraid of? What is he looking at?
sceneMetadata.whatChanged: Mara and Eli reunite. The tension between them is established.

children.title: The Document
children.content:
The file was older than Mara had expected. Not digital—physical. A manila folder, the kind she associated with old films about government conspiracies, its edges soft from handling.

Eli had placed it on the kitchen table between them like an offering. Or a warning.

She had not attended the funeral. This was a fact they had not yet spoken about, and she could feel it in the room like a third presence.

She opened the folder.
children.synopsis: Eli shows Mara a document found in their late father's belongings that changes everything Mara thought she knew.
children.notes: The contents of the document need to be decided. Options: (a) evidence of a crime, (b) a will that disinherits someone, (c) a family secret. Currently leaning toward (c).
children.label: purple

sceneMetadata.unansweredQuestions: What exactly is in the document? Who else knows?
sceneMetadata.whatChanged: The central mystery is revealed. Mara can no longer remain uninvolved.

children.title: The Witness
children.content: The woman's name was Adaeze, and she was the one who had first found the irregularities. She told Mara this over coffee in a place that served coffee so bad it had to be deliberate—some kind of leveller, Mara thought, a space where everyone arrived as equals in suffering.
children.synopsis: Mara meets Adaeze, the woman who first uncovered the secret and brought it to their father years ago.
children.notes: Adaeze is a crucial secondary character. Her arc needs more development. She should feel like a protagonist in her own right, not just an information-delivery mechanism.
children.label: green

sceneMetadata.unansweredQuestions: How much does Adaeze actually know? Is she safe?
sceneMetadata.whatChanged: Mara learns her father was actively involved in the secret, not just a passive recipient.

children.title: Eli Disappears
children.content:
He was not at the apartment when she returned. His phone went to voicemail after two rings—the specific pattern of someone declining the call, not someone unavailable. His jacket was gone. The folder was still on the kitchen table.

Mara sat down on the floor of his kitchen and tried to remember the last thing she knew for certain.

She had arrived. He had been frightened. He had shown her the folder.

He had said: Don't tell anyone you have this.

She had said: I don't have it, you do.

He had said nothing.
children.synopsis: Mara returns to find Eli gone, his phone declining her calls — the stakes shift from personal to dangerous.
children.notes: This is the end of Act 2. The final image (Mara on the kitchen floor) needs to be strong. Maybe the folder should be open when she returns.
children.label: red

sceneMetadata.unansweredQuestions: Where is Eli? Is he hiding or was he taken?
sceneMetadata.whatChanged: Mara is now alone with the secret. Eli's absence escalates stakes to danger.

fragments.title: The smell of Eli's building
fragments.content: Damp concrete and something fried. The smell of a building that had never expected to last this long and had stopped pretending otherwise. There was also, beneath it, the sweet rot of old wood — somewhere a window frame had given up its caulk, and the rain had been getting in since at least last spring.
fragments.possiblePlacement: Could expand Scene 2 (Eli's Apartment) — building description

fragments.title: Adaeze on her own family
fragments.content: "My mother used to say: a secret shared is a secret halved. She was wrong. A secret shared is a secret that now has two points of failure instead of one." Adaeze looked at her coffee cup. "I've thought about that a lot lately."
fragments.possiblePlacement: Scene 4 (The Witness) — during the café conversation

fragments.title: Mara's internal monologue on returning
fragments.content: There is a particular cruelty in returning to a place you left in anger. The anger keeps perfectly. The place changes. You are left standing in the gap between what you remember and what exists, arguing with architecture.
fragments.possiblePlacement: Scene 1 (The Arrival) — as internal reflection on the platform

omittedMaterial.title: The Prologue — Father's Perspective
omittedMaterial.content:
He had known this day would come. A man who carries a document like this carries it knowing that eventually someone else will have to carry it too.

He thought about his daughter, the older one, the one who had left. She would come back for something like this. People always came back for something.

He put the folder in the bottom drawer of his desk and covered it with a copy of a tax return from 2003. He told himself it was hiding. He knew it was passing the burden on.
omittedMaterial.reason: Cut prologue — switched to Mara's POV opening. Father's chapter felt like it pre-empted the mystery.
omittedMaterial.notes: Might work as a flashback in later acts if the father's interiority needs to be established.

omittedMaterial.title: Mara's phone call to a friend
omittedMaterial.content:
She called Petra from the station concourse.

"I'm back," she said.

"I know," Petra said. "Your brother told me you were coming."

Mara looked at a departure board. Platform 4, delayed. "He called you?"

"He calls me sometimes. We're friends."

This was new information delivered as though it were not. Mara filed it away.
omittedMaterial.reason: Removed — introduced Petra too early and deflected from Mara's isolation which is the point of the scene.
omittedMaterial.notes: Petra could still be introduced later as a secondary character — this dialogue could be repurposed.

notebookEntries.title: On the mystery at the heart of this book
notebookEntries.content:
I keep avoiding deciding what the document actually says. I've been hiding behind the idea that the mystery should feel open during drafting so I don't write toward a fixed solution.

But I think this is avoidance. The reader needs to feel the weight of what Mara is reading, and I can't evoke weight without knowing what it is.

Options I keep returning to:
1. The father was a witness to something criminal and didn't report it
2. There's another sibling — illegitimate — who doesn't know they're a sibling
3. The father's identity itself is falsified — he's not who he said he was

Option 3 feels most like the book I'm trying to write. The estrangement takes on a different character if Mara is estranged from someone who was also estranged — from himself, from his origins.

notebookEntries.title: Research — immigrant communities and document falsification
notebookEntries.content:
Looked into how identity documents were handled for displaced persons post-WWII. There are cases of people who survived by adopting the identities of those who didn't. This was sometimes with full knowledge of authorities and sometimes not.

For the novel: if the father arrived under a false name, it might mean:
- His original family name is unknown to Mara and Eli
- He may have surviving relatives under his real name who don't know he exists
- Mara's legal identity might be based on a fiction

This gives the document enormous weight. It's not just a secret — it's the ground beneath the whole family.

notebookEntries.title: Character note — Adaeze's background (private)
notebookEntries.content:
Adaeze is not just the witness. She's been carrying this for longer than Mara's father was. She found the irregularities seven years ago — before Mara left, before Eli's paranoia started.

She's been watching Eli's mental state deteriorate and wondering if it's her fault for telling him.

This should not be in the text directly. But it needs to be under the text, informing every word she says.

codexEntries.name: Mara Voss
codexEntries.description: The protagonist. Mid-thirties, has spent six years working abroad as a translator (German, French, Portuguese) after a falling-out with her family following her father's behaviour at her mother's funeral.
codexEntries.notes: Her profession as translator is not accidental — she spends her life finding the closest possible equivalent for something, never quite the thing itself. Apply this to her relationships.
codexEntries.relationships: Estranged sister to Eli Voss. Daughter of the late father. No current romantic relationship.
codexEntries.physicalDetails: Medium height. Dark hair she keeps short for convenience. Wears the same coat she bought in Berlin in 2019.
codexEntries.voiceNotes: Speaks in precise, slightly formal register. Trained eye for wrong words. Will say 'inaccurate' rather than 'wrong'.
codexEntries.arcNotes: Moves from detachment-as-protection to reengagement. Her arc is about learning that precision is not the same as truth.
codexEntries.secrets: She was present at the argument that led to her leaving but has revised the memory in ways she doesn't recognize as revision.
codexEntries.contradictions: Claims not to care about the family but has kept every letter Eli sent (unopened).

codexEntries.name: The Father
codexEntries.description: Recently deceased. Present only in memory, the document, and the reactions of others. His true name may not be his original name.
codexEntries.notes: He should feel like a person, not a plot device. The temptation is to make him entirely guilty or entirely sympathetic — resist both.
codexEntries.relationships: Father of Mara and Eli. Widower. May have family he doesn't acknowledge.
codexEntries.physicalDetails: Only seen in photographs described by others.
codexEntries.voiceNotes: Never directly quoted. His voice comes through other characters' impressions.
codexEntries.arcNotes: His arc is revealed retroactively. He made a choice to bury the document rather than act on it.
codexEntries.secrets: His identity. What he knew. What he chose not to do.
codexEntries.contradictions: Described by Eli as a coward and by Adaeze as the only person she trusted.

codexEntries.name: Adaeze Okafor
codexEntries.description: Secondary protagonist. A forensic accountant who discovered financial irregularities that led her to the document. Has been living with this knowledge for seven years.
codexEntries.notes: She is not a victim. Do not write her as one. She made an active choice to bring this to the father, and she lives with the consequences of that choice.
codexEntries.relationships: Professional acquaintance of the late father. Now in contact with Mara.
codexEntries.physicalDetails: Methodical in her appearance. Reads as calm; is not.
codexEntries.voiceNotes: Speaks in the register of someone who has rehearsed what they're going to say and then deviated from the script.
codexEntries.arcNotes: She needs to decide whether to go further or protect herself. Currently she is doing both simultaneously.
codexEntries.secrets: She has a copy of the document. She hasn't told Mara this.
codexEntries.contradictions: Claims she only came forward for ethical reasons. Has also protected herself throughout.

questions.text: What exactly does the document reveal about the father's identity?
questions.notes: This is the central question. All other plot points depend on its answer. See notebook entry 'On the mystery at the heart of this book' for options.

questions.text: What is the father's original name and where did he actually come from?
questions.notes: Research into displaced persons documentation might inform this. See research notebook entry.

questions.text: Does Adaeze have a copy of the document, and when does she reveal this to Mara?
questions.notes: Per codex entry, she does have a copy. The dramatic question is timing — does Mara find out before or after Eli disappears?

moodboardItems.title: Eastern European railway stations at dusk
moodboardItems.description: The atmosphere I'm reaching for in Scene 1. Not quite Germany, not quite Eastern Europe — somewhere in between. High ceilings, muted light, the feeling of transition as a physical condition.
moodboardItems.notes: The train platform should feel monumental and indifferent simultaneously.

moodboardItems.title: Brutalist residential building in winter
moodboardItems.description: Eli's building. Something that was built with utopian intentions and has been lived in past the utopia. The architecture that outlasted its ideology.
moodboardItems.notes: This might be too iconic — consider a less recognisable image. The goal is atmosphere, not reference.

history.description: Snapshot created: "Original draft — building description only"
